coredata.dc:title: Construction of cyclodextrin functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot electrochemical sensing interface for recognition of tryptophan isomers
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Fang
Pei, Hebing
Jia, Qianqian
Guo, Wei
Zhang, Xuejiao
Guo, Ruibin
Liu, Nijuan
Mo, Zunli
coredata.dc:description: Amino acids are the most common class of enantiomers, and they are also an indispensable component of life. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared by a top-down method. And then amide reaction was used to covalently graft aminated β-cyclodextrin (NH2-β-CD) to the edge of NGQDs. By preparing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (β-CD-NGQDs) and applying them to the glassy carbon electrode to construct an electrochemical sensing interface, enantiomers of tryptophan can be effectively identified. NGQDs can effectively improve the conductivity of β-CD. When chiral selectors interact with enantiomers, NGQDs can provide additional interactions, such as hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions to indirectly enhancing chiral recognition capabilities. β-Cyclodextrin has the ability to combine with a variety of guest molecules to form composite materials, including some amino acid molecules with different configurations. The microscopic morphology of the material was characterized by a transmission electron microscope, and it was found that the β-CD-NGQDs chiral composite material has good film-forming properties, which is beneficial to the formation of a sensing interface. At the same time, its composition and structure were also identified by X-ray powder diffraction, infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, ultraviolet spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The β-CD-NGQDs was drip-coated on the glassy carbon electrode to form a working electrode. The tested differential pulse voltammetry curves of different configurations of tryptophan showed that the chiral composite material can recognize l-tryptophan.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-Cyclodextrin
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor
Tryptophan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

Gansu Province Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51262027 , 21861034 ); the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province ( 20YF3GA022 ); the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province of China ( 18JR3RA094 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of NGQDs.
Schematic diagram of synthesis of β-CD-NGQDs by EDC/NHS method.
(a) (b)TEM images of NGQDs at different magnifications. (c) (d) TEM images of β-CD-NGQDs at different magnifications.

(a) XRD characterization patterns of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs; (b) (B) FT-IR characterization map of NH2-β-CD, NGQDs, and β-CD-NGQDs; (c) Raman spectra of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs; (d) UV–vis spectra of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of β-CD-NGQDs; (b) C1s regional peak splitting map of β-CD-NGQDs; (c) O1s regional peak splitting map of β-CD-NGQDs; (d) N1s region peak map of β-CD-NGQDs.
Photographs of water contact angles of GCE surface modified by different materials (a) bare GCE, (b) NH2-β-CD/GCE, (c) NGQDs/GCE, (d) β-CD-NGQDs/GCE.

(a) CV curve of different working electrodes in 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution; (b) Nyquist diagram and circuit fitting diagram; (c) β-CD- NGQDs/GCE CV curves of at different scanning speeds; (d) The linear relationship between cathode peak and anode peak current values at different scanning speeds.
(a) DPV of naked GCE, (b) NGQDs/GCE, (c) NH2-β-CD/GCE and (d) β-CD-NGQDs/GCE in 5 mM L-Trp and D-Trp solutions Graph; (e) GCE modified by different materials to identify the histogram of Trp isomer.

(a) The binding model of β-CD-NGQDs and L-Trp. (b) The binding model of β-CD-NGQDs and D-Trp. In the figure, the blue dotted line is the hydrogen bond interaction, the green sticks model is L-Trp molecule, and the white sticks model is a β-CD-NGQDs molecule, in which the O, H, and N atoms are unified as red, white, and blue. (c) Comparison of β-CD-NGQDs and L-Trp/D-Trp binding model. (Red: L-Trp, blue: D-Trp). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The influence of incubation time on the peak current of differential pulse voltammetry curve for identifying different Trp isomers; (b) The influence of different pH on the peak current of differential pulse voltammetry for identifying different Trp isomers.
Hydrogen bond data formed when β-CD-NGQDs bind to L-Trp.
Hydrogen bond data formed when β-CD-NGQDs bind to D-Trp.

head.ce:title: Construction of cyclodextrin functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot electrochemical sensing interface for recognition of tryptophan isomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials
Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amino acids are the most common class of enantiomers, and they are also an indispensable component of life. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared by a top-down method. And then amide reaction was used to covalently graft aminated β-cyclodextrin (NH2-β-CD) to the edge of NGQDs. By preparing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (β-CD-NGQDs) and applying them to the glassy carbon electrode to construct an electrochemical sensing interface, enantiomers of tryptophan can be effectively identified. NGQDs can effectively improve the conductivity of β-CD. When chiral selectors interact with enantiomers, NGQDs can provide additional interactions, such as hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions to indirectly enhancing chiral recognition capabilities. β-Cyclodextrin has the ability to combine with a variety of guest molecules to form composite materials, including some amino acid molecules with different configurations. The microscopic morphology of the material was characterized by a transmission electron microscope, and it was found that the β-CD-NGQDs chiral composite material has good film-forming properties, which is beneficial to the formation of a sensing interface. At the same time, its composition and structure were also identified by X-ray powder diffraction, infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, ultraviolet spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The β-CD-NGQDs was drip-coated on the glassy carbon electrode to form a working electrode. The tested differential pulse voltammetry curves of different configurations of tryptophan showed that the chiral composite material can recognize l-tryptophan.

ce:section.ce:para: As the molecular building blocks of life, amino acids are all chiral molecules except for glycine, and the isomers of amino acids exhibit different biological activities [1,2]. The recognition and separation of L- and d-amino acids are essential processes from the viewpoints of scientific understanding and practical application [3]. The electrochemical technologies, which can offer many advantages over the other conventional methods, have received considerable attention in chiral recognition [4]. Tryptophan is an important type of amino acid. It has been reported in the literature to synthesize electrochemical chiral sensing materials for the identification of tryptophan enantiomers [5,6].

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are highly heterogeneous zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials derived from graphene with a graphene framework structure of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon atoms [7]. Graphene nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 100 nm are called GQDs. The nanometer-scale diameter gives GQDs a size effect and quantum confinement effect. The oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups contained in the structure of GQDs make it easy to dissolve in organic solvents, and at the same time make it useful for functionalization. When the edge effect of GQDs is synergistic or combined with peripheral functional groups, it can produce a strong and stable photoluminescence phenomenon [8]. In recent years, the research on GQDs have mainly focused on photoluminescence [9–11]. At the same time, excellent chemical, mechanical, low toxicity and biocompatibility make graphene quantum dots one of the focuses of attention in the research of new carbon materials [12], and they have also been regarded as research hotspots in many scientific fields. It has greatly promoted the new progress of this carbon nanomaterial in biosensing, biomedical imaging, drug delivery, and electronic devices [13]. The characteristics of GQDs also make them have application value in electrochemical sensing.

Compared with simple GQDs, the doping of N atoms in NGQDs can significantly adjust the electronic and chemical environment of the conjugated sp2 domain [14,15], making the surface and edges have abundant structural defects, which can significantly improve the conductivity of GQDs. Although GQDs have been applied to electrochemical sensing [16], however, due to the lack of chiral sites in GQDs, there are few reports on the recognition of enantiomers based on GQDs, and reports on the recognition of enantiomers based on NGQDs less. Recently, Wu [17] et al. reported that graphene quantum dots functionalized cyclodextrin and cellulose chiral stationary phases have enhanced enantiomer separation performance, which indicates that GQDs-based enantiomer recognition is feasible Yes, and NGQDs is an extension of GQDs, so using NGQDs as functional materials to manufacture chiral electrochemical sensors is still an innovative work.

In supramolecular systems, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) has become an ideal host material for accommodating various guest molecules due to its truncated pyramidal hydrophilic outer surface and hydrophobic central cavity [18] and has a stable and the most commonly used chiral selector β-CD with high saturation and high saturation. Researchers have proved that it can perform stereoselective recognition of chiral substances [19–22], but the poor conductivity and good water solubility make it difficult for us to distinguish β-CD. CD is directly fixed on the electrode surface, thus limiting the electrochemical application of β-CD in the field of chirality. Therefore, we need to find a suitable substrate material and compound it with β-CDs to overcome the defects of β-CD. A dual signal electrochemical sensing strategy was proposed by Zhu et al. for the first time in chiral recognition based on the competitive host–guest interaction between probe and chiral isomers with β-CD/CNTs@rGO [23].

In this paper, NGQDs were prepared by a top-down method, and then β-CD functionalized NGQDs (β-CD-NGQDs) were successfully synthesized by chemical grafting of amide reaction between amino and carboxyl. Dropping β-CD-NGQDs onto the glassy carbon electrode to construct an electrochemical sensing interface through self-assembly, which can effectively carry out tryptophan (Trp) chiral recognition of enantiomers. NGQDs can effectively improve the conductivity of β-CD, and when chiral selectors interact with enantiomers, NGQDs can provide additional interactions, such as hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions, thereby indirectly enhancing the chiral recognition ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared by the acid oxidation cutting method according to our previous literature reports [24,25]. As shown in Fig. 1 , 0.2 g reduced graphene oxide and 200 mL concentrated HNO3 were added into a flask, install a condensing device, and keep stirring at 150 °C for 24 h to obtain a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot dispersion. Disassemble the condensing device until the liquid completely evaporates to obtain solid graphene quantum dots. The solid graphene quantum dots are dissolved in deionized water, and impurities are removed by filtration and dialysis.

The graphene quantum dots prepared by the strong acid oxidation method have more oxygen-containing groups at the edges, mainly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Considering its structural characteristics, as shown in Fig. 2 , the EDC/NHS method was used to synthesize β-cyclodextrin functional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. First, 50 mg NGQDs and 50 mL H2O were mixed and sonicated for 1 h, and then 50 mg EDC and 50 mg NHS were added to activate the carboxyl groups at the edge of NGQDs to prepare a 1 mg mL−1 NGQDs dispersion. After sonication for 30 min, 1 mg mL−1 of NH2-β-CD was added (100 mg NH2-β-CD was added to 100 mL of H2O). After stirring for 48 h at room temperature, the solution was evaporated in a rotary evaporator, and the product was collected and freeze-dried.

Disperse 4 mg of β-CD-NGQDs in 4 mL of deionized water and treat with low power ultrasonic for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension of 1.0 mg mL−1. The glass carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3 mm was first polished with 1.0 μm aluminum oxide (Al2O3) polishing powder, and then with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm Al2O3 polishing powder to remove impurities on the GCE surface. To remove the remaining Al2O3 polishing powder, the GCE was first sonicated in absolute ethanol for 2 min, then sonicated in deionized water for 2 min, and dried in a nitrogen stream for later using. Use a pipette to take 8.0 μL of 1.0 mg mL−1 β-CD-NGQDs uniform suspension and drop it on the surface of the GCE electrode, and dry it under an infrared lamp to form a β-CD-NGQDs modified glassy carbon electrode, which is expressed as β-CD-NGQDs/GCE. For comparison experiments, 1.0 mg mL-1 of NGQDs and β-CD dispersions were prepared, and similarly, the two were dropped on the surface of GCE to form β-CD/GCE and NGQDs/GCE.

Prepare 100 mL of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution, take 25 mL in the electrolytic cell, use saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, glassy carbon electrode modified with different materials As the working electrode, the cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested under the three-electrode system to evaluate the electrochemical performance of different working electrodes. Place the β-CD-NGQDs modified glassy carbon electrode in a pre-configured 5 mM L-Trp and D-Trp solution with pH = 6, the incubation time is 20 s, and the scan voltage is set between 0.4 and 1.2 V Under the conditions of a pulse width of 0.1 s and a pulse amplitude of 50 mV, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is used to identify electrochemical chirality through changes in peak current intensity. The differential pulse voltammetry curves of NGQDs modified glassy carbon electrode and NH2-β-CD modified glassy carbon electrode in tryptophan solution were tested under the same conditions.

To study the mechanism of β-CD-NGQDs recognition of tryptophan isomers, we mainly used the Lamarckian genetic algorithm of the AutoDock Vina program for molecular docking simulation research. The structure files of the target and target small molecules (Lig1, Lig2, shown in Fig. S1) are drawn by Chem3D software, and the MM2 force field is used for energy minimization and structure optimization. First, use the AutoDockTools software to perform operations such as adding H atoms, adding Gasteiger-Hücker empirical charges, merging non-polar hydrogens, and setting rotatable bonds to the structure files of the target and target small molecules. During the docking process, the AutoDock Vina program placed a square docking box with a size of 30 × 30 × 30 Å and a grid spacing of 0.375 Å in the center of the target to cover the entire target. Within the range of the docking box, the Lamarckian genetic algorithm generates 50 random orientations and random small molecule conformations. Each round of passaging undergoes a maximum of 1,500,000 energy optimizations, and finally, the calculation is terminated after 50,000 generations of optimization. Other parameters used to run the AutoDock Vina program are default values. Finally, we selected the best binding confirmation from the docking results, and analyzed the intermolecular interaction through the Protein-Ligand Interaction Profiler online server, extracted key interaction parameters such as bond length and bond angle, and used Pymol software to draw a three-dimensional interaction diagram.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) are transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images of NGQDs at different magnifications, respectively. From Fig. 3 (a), it can be seen that the size of NGQDs is about 10 nm, some of which are monodisperse, and a small amount of agglomeration (two or three monodisperse NGQDs gather together to form slightly larger particles). Fig. 3 (b) is the HR-TEM image of NGQDs. A monodisperse NGQDs with a clear outline can be seen in the field of view, and obvious lattice fringes corresponding to the graphite d (1120) lattice plane can be observed. The lattice spacing is 0.241 nm [26].

Fig. 3 (c) and (d) are TEM images of β-CD-NGQDs at different magnifications. Due to the poor dispersibility of β-CD-NGQDs prepared by covalent grafting in ethanol, the β-CD-NGQDs were dispersed in water to test TEM, and the aqueous dispersion of β-CD-NGQDs was dropped on the ultra-thin carbon film Test after drying under infrared light. In the TEM image, we can see that the monodisperse β-CD-NGQDs are almost non-existent. The β-CD-NGQDs self-assembled on the ultra-thin carbon film to form a uniform film, which is different from the previous study [27].

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) is a commonly used characterization method for analyzing the structure of substances. Fig. 4 (a) is the XRD spectra of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs. In the figure, the peaks of NGQDs at 10.8° and 26.5° are characteristic diffraction peaks of the graphene structure. The peak at 10.8° indicates that the graphene is oxidized. Hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other oxygen-containing groups are inserted in the middle of the carbon layer, which suggests the existence of p-p stacking between graphene sheets and the residual of oxygen containing functional groups in blank Graphene [28,29], and the peak at 26.5° is attributed to the (002) crystal plane of graphene, indicating that the crystal structure of graphene has no crystal structure during the synthesis of NGQD was destroyed [30]. Comparing the characteristic diffraction peaks of the two, it can be found that the peak intensity of β-CD-NGQDs at 26.5° is weakened, and a new peak appears at 19.6°, which is the characteristic diffraction peak of β-cyclodextrin [31]. Taken together, after β-cyclodextrin is functionalized, the crystalline form of NGQDs has undergone some changes, indicating a change in its molecular structure.

As a modern instrumental analysis method, infrared spectroscopy (IR) has been widely used in the study of molecular structure and chemical composition. Fig. 4 (b) is the infrared spectrum of NGQDs, NH2-β-CD, and β-CD-NGQDs. It can be seen that the stretching vibration peaks of the O–H and N–H bonds appears at 3400 cm−1, the stretching vibration peaks of the C–H bonds are located at 2932 cm−1, which are attributed to the methylene group (-CH2), and the C–O stretching vibration peak of the bond is at 1036 cm−1, which corresponds to the alcohol compound. It can also be observed that the peak at 1710 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of the carbonyl group (CO), and the peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to the deformation vibration of the N–H bond. The peak at 1387 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of C–O, and the peak at 1227 cm−1 is also the stretching vibration peak of C–N, but it is attributed to the mid-CN stretching vibration of the amide bond. In NGQDs, it is obvious that the three peaks at 1710 cm−1, 1387 cm−1, and 1227 cm−1 can initially prove that NGQDs are successfully synthesized, and there are two main forms of N atom doping, one is to replace graphite the position of the C atom in the benzene ring inside the alkene quantum dot, and the second is doped into the edge of the graphene quantum dot. Comparing the infrared spectra of NGQDs, NH2-β-CD, and β-CD-NGQDs, it is found that N–H and CO bond at 3400 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, and 1640 cm−1 appear in β-CD-NGQDs, basically, it can be explained that an amide bond is formed between NGQDs and NH2-β-CD, and the synthesis of chiral recognition materials is successful.

Raman spectroscopy is a commonly used analytical method when studying molecular structure. Fig. 4 (c) shows the Raman spectra of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs. The figure clearly shows two characteristic peaks, the D band peak at 1350 cm−1 and the G band peak at 1574 cm−1. It is well known that D-band peaks and G-band peaks are the Raman characteristic peaks of carbon atom crystals. D-band represents disordered carbon atoms in carbon materials, and G-band represents the first-order scattering E2g vibration mode, which is used to represent sp2 heterogeneity. The characteristic peak of the in-plane stretching vibration of the carbon atom. The intensity ratio of D-band and G-band is a measure of disordered graphite [32–34]. From Fig. 4 (c), the intensity ratio of D band and G band in NGQDs ID/IG = 0.965, the intensity ratio of D band and G band in β-CD-NGQDs ID/IG = 0.945, ID/IG value larger the value, it indicates that there are more defects in the carbon atom crystals, so I can know that NGQDs have more disordered carbon atoms than β-CD-NGQDs. In comparison, after β-CD is covalently grafted to NGQDs, the content of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in the system has increased, which is consistent with the results of Raman spectroscopy, so it can be confirmed from the side that the chiral recognition material is successfully prepared.

Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy is one of the commonly used methods to analyze and characterize the composition, content, and structure of substances. Fig. 4 (d) is the UV–visible spectra of NGQDs and β-CD-NGQDs. The absorption peak at 290 nm in the figure is mainly derived from the n→π* transition of nonbonded electrons containing oxygen groups and sp2 hybrid atoms, And the maximum absorption peak at 220–230 nm can be attributed to the π→π* transition in the polyaromatic system (ie CC bond) in the chiral graphene quantum dot structure [35,36].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technology can perform qualitative, quantitative, or semi-quantitative and valence analysis on the elemental composition of solid samples. Fig. S2 shows the XPS full spectrum of NGQDs and the peak fitting diagram of each element. In the full spectrum of NGQDs, the presence of C, N, and O elements can be seen, of which C element accounts for 67.61 %, and the N element accounted for 4.10 %, and the O element accounted for 28.29 %, which proved that N atoms were successfully doped into graphene quantum dots. Fig. S2 (b) is the C1s spectrum of NGQDs. It can be seen from the figure that there are four main forms of C element. The peak at 284.62 eV is attributed to the CC bond, and the peak at 285.12 eV corresponds to the CC bond. The peak at 285.62 eV is caused by the C–N bond, while the peak at 288.99 belongs to O–CO [37,38]. The existence of the CC bond indicates that NGQDs have a benzene ring structure. Fig. S2 (c) is the O 1s spectrum of NGQDs. The peak at binding energy equal to 531.61 eV belongs to the O–H bond, and the peak at 532.49 eV is attributed to the C= O bond, the peak at 533.74 eV corresponds to the CO–O–C bond. Combining the C1s and O1s spectra show that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots have a benzene ring structure and are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. Fig. S2 (d) is the N1s spectrum of NGQDs. From the peak fitting results in the figure, the peak at 399.86 eV belongs to pyridine N, the peak at 400.62 eV belongs to pyrrole N, and the peak at 401.37 eV belongs to graphite the peak at 402.12 eV is due to the presence of N(N+) in the oxidation state [39]. The analysis of XPS results of NGQDs showed that in the process of synthesizing graphene quantum dots using concentrated nitric acid oxidative cutting, N atoms in concentrated nitric acid were doped into the structure of graphene quantum dots. This result is consistent with the results obtained by infrared spectroscopy.

Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technology to perform elemental quantitative analysis and valence analysis on the composition of β-cyclodextrin-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The existence of C, N, and O elements can also be seen in the full spectrum of β-CD-NGQDs, of which C element accounts for 79.94 %, N element accounts for 2.53 %, and O element accounts for 17.53 %. Compare the XPS full spectrum of NGQDs Quantitative analysis of each element can find that the content of N in β-CD-NGQDs is reduced, which is mainly because NH2-β-CD is used in the experiment to carry out amidation coupling reaction with NGQDs, while N in NH2-β-CD The content of elements is only 1.23 %, most of which are C elements, and the rest are O elements. Therefore, the content of N elements in the new β-CD-NGQDs generated by the reaction will decrease, which explains the formation of amide bonds from the side. Fig. 5 (b) is the C1s spectrum of β-CD-NGQDs. It can be seen from the figure that there are four main forms of C element. The peak at 284.62 eV is attributed to the CC bond, and the peak at 285.12 eV corresponds to the C =C bond, the peak at 285.62 eV is caused by the CN bond, while the peak at 288.99 eV belongs to O–CO [40]. Fig. 5 (c) is the O1s spectrum of β-CD-NGQDs. The peak at binding energy equal to 531.61 eV belongs to the OH bond, the peak at 532.49 eV is attributed to the CO bond, and the peak at 533.74 eV corresponds to C–O–C bond [41], combined with C1s and O1s spectra, shows that β-CD-NGQDs has a benzene ring structure and also has abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. Fig. 5 (d) is the N1s spectrum of β-CD-NGQDs. From the peak fitting results in the figure, the peak at 399.74 eV belongs to pyridine N, the peak at 400.45 eV belongs to pyrrole N, and the peak at 401.36 eV the peak is attributed to graphitized N, and the peak at 402.37 eV is due to the presence of oxidation state N (N+) [42].

The water contact angle experiment can measure the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of different materials. In the experiments in this chapter, we tested the water contact angle of bare GCE, NGQDs, and β-CD-NGQDs modified GCE (as shown in Fig. 6 ). Each test is to drop 2 μL drops onto the sample. It can be clearly observed from the figure that the water contact angle of the bare glassy carbon electrode is 59.35°. When the glassy carbon electrode is modified by NH2-β-CD, the hydrophilic angle drops to 39.96°. This is because β-CD has the inner cavity is hydrophobic and the outer is hydrophilic. After modification of GCE, its hydrophilicity is imposed. Fig. 6 (c) is a photo of the water contact angle test of GCE modified by NGQDs. It can be seen that compared to GCE without modification and modified with other materials, its hydrophilic angle is the largest. This is because the graphene quantum dots prepared by the strong acid oxidation cutting method There are abundant oxygen-containing groups on the edge of the, so its hydrophilicity is also obvious. The GCE modified by NH2-β-CD covalently grafted with NGQDs has a larger hydrophilic angle than GCE modified by NGQDs, which is 35.36°, but smaller than GCE modified by NH2-β-CD.

Using cyclic voltammetry (CV) to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of glassy carbon electrodes modified with different materials as working electrodes in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution of 0.1 M KCl, the measured voltage range is −0.2V-0.6 V, the sweep speed is 50 mV s−1, the result is shown in Fig. 7 (a). It can be seen from the figure that unmodified GCE, NGQDs modified GCE, and β-CD-NGQDs modified GCE has a set of obvious redox peaks, which are due to [Fe(CN)6]3- and [Fe(CN)6]4- The transfer of electrons between them indicates that the reaction on the electrode is reversible. Compared with the unmodified GCE, the intensity of the redox peak of NGQDs/GCE has a certain increase, and the peak current value of the redox peak of β-CD-NGQDs/GCE is the largest. Calculate according to Randles-Sevcĭk formula (1):
(1) Ip = 2.69 × 105n3/2AD1/2v1/2C

In this formula, Ip is the peak current value of the anode, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the effective area of the working electrode, D is the diffusion coefficient, v is the scan rate, and C (mol mL-1) is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]4-/3-. In a [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution containing 0.1 M KCl, n = 1 and D = 7.6 × 10-6 cm2 s−1. Using this equation, the effective working areas of the three different working electrodes, bare GCE, NGQDs/GCE, and β-CD-NGQDs/GCE, are 4.92 mm2, 5.52 mm2, and 6.04 mm2, respectively.

To further study the advantages of different composite materials as electrode materials, in the frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz, when the amplitude is 5 mV, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy can also explain the electrochemical behavior of different electrodes. Fig. 7 (b) is the Nyquist diagram of different electrodes. You can observe the semicircle in the high-frequency region and the approximate straight line in the low-frequency region. The intersection of the extension line of the low-frequency region and the X-axis represents the equivalent series resistance (Rs) in the series model, which is the sum of the resistance of the electrolyte solution, the contact resistance of the electrode/electrolyte interface, and the inherent resistance of the electrode material. The diameter of the high-frequency region represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), and Cdl represents the double-layer capacitance. The straight line in the low-frequency region represents the diffusion behavior of ions in the electrode hole (Zw). The steeper the line, the faster the diffusion of ions in the electrolyte. From the fitting result, we can get the Rct value as follows: bare GCE > NGQDs/GCE> β-CD-NGQDs/GCE.

The dynamics of the electrode surface has a great influence on the recognition of the chiral characteristics. The change of the peak current value in the cyclic voltammetry curve at different scanning speeds can be used to study the dynamic mechanism of the electrode surface. From Fig. 7 (c), it can be observed that after β-CD-NGQDs modified GCE, the scanning speed gradually increased from 10 mV s−1 to 150 mV s−1, and the cathodic peak increased in the direction of positive potential. Moving, the anode peak moves in the direction of increasing negative potential [43]. The peak current values of the cathode peak and anode peak and the square root of the scanning speed obtained at different scanning speeds are listed in Fig. 7 (d). It can be clearly seen that there is a linear relationship between the peak current value and the square root of the scanning speed, namely It shows that the electrochemical behavior that occurs on the electrode is controlled by the diffusion process. The linear relationship of the anode peak current fitting can be expressed as Ipa = 6.881v1/2–0.598, the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9975, and the linear relationship of the cathode peak current fitting can be expressed as Ipc = −5.621v1/2-6.428, linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9828.

Bare GCE, NGQDs/GCE, and β-CD-NGQDs/GCE were placed in 25 mL of 5 mM L-Trp and D-Trp solutions (pH = 6.0), respectively, and then incubate for 20 s. After the pulse width is 0.1 s, the pulse amplitude Under the conditions of 50 mV and a scanning voltage of −0.2–0.6 V, the electrochemical chiral identification of tryptophan isomers was performed by differential pulse voltammetry. It can be seen from Fig. 8 (a) that the peak current values of naked GCE in L-Trp and D-Trp solutions are almost the same, and the chiral recognition efficiency is only 1.019. After using NGQDs to modify the glassy carbon electrode in Fig. 8 (b), the peak current values in the L-Trp and D-Trp solutions are slightly different, and the chiral recognition efficiency is only 1.067. In Fig. 8 (c), it is obvious that the peak current value of NH2-β-CD/GCE in the L-Trp solution is higher than the peak current value in the D-Trp solution. This is because of β-CD is a chiral selective agent, L-Trp can enter its cavity, but the recognition efficiency of a single β-CD is not high, only 1.169. As shown in Fig. 8 (d), the peak current generated by β-CD-NGQDs/GCE in L-Trp solution is significantly higher than the peak current generated by β-CD-NGQDs/GCE in D-Trp solution. The calculated peak current ratio (IL/ID) of CD-NGQDs/L-Trp and β-CD-NGQDs/D-Trp is 2.569. This result shows that, compared with D-Trp, since it is easier to form H bonds between the secondary hydroxyl groups on the edge of β-CD and the amino groups of L-Trp, β-CD preferentially contains L-Trp into its cavity [26,41]. Interestingly, although there is a tapered hydrophobic cavity that can contain various guest molecules in β-CD, the enantiomeric recognition efficiency (IL/ID) of NH2-β-CD modified GCE is significantly lower than β-CD-NGQDs modified GCE. The decreased recognition efficiency of NH2-β-CD modified GCE may be attributed to the following fact: due to the dielectric and insulating properties of NH2-β-CD, its electrochemical signal is relatively weak, and the peak current value difference is not obvious.

The hydroxyl groups on the edge of β-CD can form hydrogen bonds with the polar groups on Trp. When cyclodextrin is used as the host molecule, the stronger the hydrophobicity of the guest molecule and the lower the polarity, the easier it is to compete with solvent molecules to form inclusion compounds. When the indolyl group with the smaller polarity of the Trp molecule enters the cyclodextrin cavity, it can form an inclusion compound of β-CD and Trp. At the same time, the more polar groups on the Trp are distributed in the edge of cyclodextrin cavity.

In the electrochemical sensing test, the chiral recognition material of the modified glassy carbon electrode and the reaction substrate, that is, amino acid isomers, require a certain time to combine, and the incubation time is too long if the chiral recognition material is easily soluble in water will fall off, which will affect the test results. Fig. 10 (a) explored the influence of the incubation time of the synthetic chiral recognition material modified glassy carbon electrode (i.e. β-CD-NGQDs/GCE) in L-Trp and D-Trp solutions on the recognition efficiency. When the incubation time increases from 10 s to 20 s, the peak current ratio IL/ID gradually increases, but when the incubation time continues to increase to 30 s, the chiral recognition efficiency does not increase, but decreases. From the previous water contact angle characterization, we know that β-CD-NGQDs are relatively hydrophilic. The amount of β-CD-NGQDs bound to Trp reaches the maximum in the first 20 s, and β-CD-NGQDs begin to fall off after 20 s. Therefore, its recognition efficiency has decreased.

To optimize the test conditions for the identification of Trp isomers by β-CD-NGQDs/GCE voltammetry on the electrochemical sensing interface of the constructed chiral composite material, the pH value of the test system was adjusted by using a phosphate buffer solution. The efficiency of chiral recognition under different pH conditions was explored. Fig. 10 (b) is the difference of β-CD-NGQDs/GCE in l-tryptophan and d-tryptophan solutions at pH = 5.7, pH = 6.0, pH = 6.5, pH = 7.0, and pH = 7.4 histogram of peak current ratio of pulse voltammetry curve. From the figure, we can see that when the pH value increases from 5.7 to 6.0, the ratio of peak current value IL/ID gradually increases, and reaches the maximum at pH = 6.0. Then it decreases as the pH increases from 6.0 to 7.4. Therefore, a buffer solution with a pH of 6.0 is used in the experiments of this work.

Supramolecular host-guest interaction provides a method for molecular binding recognition through non-covalent interaction. β-Cyclodextrin is one of the most widely studied host molecules. It can combine with a variety of guest molecules to form complexes, including some amino acid molecules with different configurations. In this chapter, amide reaction is used to covalently graft aminated β-cyclodextrin to the edge of NGQDs, forming a new molecule, namely β-CD-NGQDs. The microscopic morphology of the material was characterized by a transmission electron microscope, and it was found that the β-CD-NGQDs chiral composite material has better film-forming properties, which is beneficial to the formation of a sensing interface. The β-CD-NGQDs was drip-coated on the glassy carbon electrode to form a working electrode, and the differential pulse voltammetry curves of different configurations of tryptophan were tested. The results showed that the chiral composite material can recognize l-tryptophan. At the same time, the calculation results of molecular docking simulation also showed that the binding force of β-CD-NGQDs to l-tryptophan was greater than that of binding to d-tryptophan, which verified the results of electrochemical chiral recognition.
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

Fang Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Hebing Pei: Data curation. Qianqian Jia: Writing – review & editing. Wei Guo: Data curation, experimental operation. Xuejiao Zhang: experimental operation. Ruibin Guo: Software. Nijuan Liu: Software. Zunli Mo: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51262027, 21861034); the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province (20YF3GA022); the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province of China (18JR3RA094).
